query: physical exam findings in patients with adenomyosis

retrieved_docs.title: Adenomyosis -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: The physical exam classically demonstrates a “boggy” enlarged uterus due to the combined effects of increased vascularization from ectopic endometrial tissue and smooth muscle proliferation. As compared to the enlarged leiomyomatous uterus, a tender uterus is more common. [15]

retrieved_docs.title: Adenomyosis -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: The clinical diagnosis of adenomyosis is difficult as a variety of common gynecologic conditions share the signs and symptoms. The most common symptoms include heavy menstrual bleeding or painful menses. Clinical diagnostic accuracy is neither sensitive nor specific. [9] Other less common symptoms include chronic pelvic pain and dyspareunia. [8] It is also crucial to note that up to 33% of women with adenomyosis may be asymptomatic. [8] While infertility is often considered a potential presenting symptom, the data is inconclusive with some sources reporting an association of 11% to 12% with adenomyosis. [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Adenomyosis -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: Adenomyosis is a problematic diagnosis due to its variable presentation and shared features with other common gynecologic conditions. The primary care clinician, obstetrician, nurse practitioner, gynecologist, diagnostic radiologist, and interventional radiologist are all possible team members who might diagnose and manage this disease. While the differential diagnosis is broad for women presenting with abnormal uterine bleeding, a transvaginal ultrasound which is the diagnostic imaging of choice for adenomyosis will likely be obtained during the initial workup. It is important to recognize that every test has its limitations to include the ultrasonographer and interpreter. It is vital that the ordering provider does not dismiss the diagnosis of adenomyosis if it is not mentioned in the report, as the diagnostic criteria have been variable for many years until the recent establishment of more standardized imaging features. [17]

retrieved_docs.title: Adenomyosis -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: The differential diagnosis of adenomyosis is broad. The signs and symptoms of adenomyosis are similar to other disease entities characterized by abnormal uterine bleeding, dysmenorrhea, or infertility. The Federal International Board of Gynecology (FIGO) classification scheme utilizes the PALM-COEIN mnemonic as a clinical tool for more consistent reporting practices. [9] [29] The mnemonic divides the diagnosis of abnormal uterine bleeding into structural and non-structural categories with each letter representing the following entities: Polyps Adenomyosis Leiomyoma Malignancy/hyperplasia Coagulopathy Ovulatory dysfunction Endometrial Iatrogenic Not yet classified Adenomyosis often co-exists with other entities within the differential diagnosis, most commonly leiomyoma (50%), endometriosis (11%), and endometrial polyps (7%). [8] [30]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Uterus -- Pathophysiology -- Adenomyosis
retrieved_docs.content: Adenomyosis is an endometriosis subtype in which endometrial tissue is implanted within the myometrium leading to chronic pain and heavy menstrual bleeding. Adenomyosis is unique because, on physical exam, the uterus will be uniformly enlarged, globular, and boggy (soft on palpation). This is in contrast to endometriosis affecting other parts of the body.

retrieved_docs.title: Adenomyosis -- Pearls and Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: Ultrasound is the preferred diagnostic imaging modality for the initial workup of adenomyosis with high sensitivity and specificity. MRI can be helpful in equivocal cases. Diagnostic imaging utilizing either ultrasound or MRI has some limitations due to numerous mimics and variable physiologic appearance of the uterus.

retrieved_docs.title: Adenomyosis -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Adenomyosis is a gynecologic condition characterized by ectopic endometrial tissue within the uterine myometrium. Presenting signs and symptoms vary, but most commonly are painful menses and/or heavy menstrual bleeding. This activity reviews the evaluation and management of adenomyosis and highlights the role of the interprofessional team in caring for patients with this condition.

retrieved_docs.title: Adenomyosis -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Review the etiology of adenomyosis. Identify the preferred diagnostic imaging modality for adenomyosis. Outline treatment considerations for patients with adenomyosis. Explain the roles of various interprofessional teams in coordinating care to improve outcomes for patients with adenomyosis. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Adenomyosis -- Evaluation -- Laboratory Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Laboratory testing is useful to rule out other disease entities included in the differential diagnosis, in addition to identifying certain complicating features such as anemia due to heavy menstruation. While some biomarkers do exist, none are specific for adenomyosis. [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Sonography Female Pelvic Pathology Assessment, Protocols, and Interpretation  -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: We discuss here and recommend reporting patterns by eminent sonography bodies in obstetrics and gynecology. Uterine pathologies like leiomyoma and adenomyosis are the most common benign pelvic pathologies. With the trending lifestyle and improved health care facilities, there is a special place for conservative management of such lesions in the case of young and infertile patients.

retrieved_docs.title: Adenomyosis -- Pearls and Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: Treatment options should target primary symptoms. NSAIDs for painful cramping, OCPs or levonorgestrel IUD for heavy bleeding, and hysterectomy for women no longer desiring fertility. Minimally invasive therapies should be a consideration in refractory cases in women who wish to preserve fertility. Adenomyosis can co-exist with leiomyoma (50%), endometriosis (11%), and endometrial polyps (7%). These associations merit attention in patients who have refractory symptoms. Up to a third of women with a sonographic diagnosis of adenomyosis may be asymptomatic. [17] While adenomyosis is classically taught as a disease of premenopausal, multiparous women, there is data showing an increased prevalence in undiagnosed younger populations. Utilization of the PALM-COEIN mnemonic can be used to broaden the differential diagnosis for abnormal uterine bleeding.

retrieved_docs.title: Adenomyosis -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: A number of mimics can have similar findings on the ultrasound exam, including tamoxifen use, prior endometrial ablation, endometriosis, uterine contractions, vascular malformations, leiomyomas, and cancer. Certain techniques such as low-frequency, coronal reconstructions, 3-D ultrasound, cine-clips, color Doppler, and saline infusion sonohysterography (SIS) can be used to differentiate between these entities.

retrieved_docs.title: Adenomyosis -- Evaluation -- MRI
retrieved_docs.content: Similar to ultrasound, a variety of mimics ranging from co-existing gynecologic pathologies to physiologic variants exist. Susceptibility weighted imaging, diffusion-weighted imaging, MR spectroscopy, cine MR imaging, and increased 3T field strength are all problem-solving strategies. [18] It is important to obtain the MRI in the late proliferative or secretory phase (days 7 to 28) due to the decreased signal of normal myometrium during the early proliferative phase (days 1 to 6). [18]

retrieved_docs.title: Adenomyosis -- Complications
retrieved_docs.content: The correlation between adenomyosis and infertility is not clearly defined. While some authors cite an association of 11% to 12%, contradictory data also exists. [8] Due to a multitude of confounders and variable diagnostic criteria for adenomyosis, no clear association has been established. [9] With more validated diagnostic imaging criteria of ultrasound in recent years, future research will be able to characterize associations between adenomyosis and infertility more confidently.

retrieved_docs.title: Adenomyosis -- Evaluation -- Transvaginal ultrasound is the preferred diagnostic imaging modality for adenomyosis. The characteristic findings reflect the histopathologic changes of the disease process and can be broken down into three categories :
retrieved_docs.content: Endometrial infiltration: Echogenic striations and nodules, myometrial cysts, and “lollipop” diverticula (cystic striations) Smooth muscle proliferation: Focal or diffuse myometrial thickening with indistinct borders more commonly involving the posterior fundus and heterogenous echotexture manifesting as “venetian blind” appearance of thin linear shadows Vascularity: Color Doppler demonstrating an increased number of tortuous vessels throughout the involved myometrium as opposed to leiomyomas which displace vessels

retrieved_docs.title: Adenomyosis -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Adenomyosis is a gynecologic condition characterized by ectopic endometrial tissue within the uterine myometrium. First described in 1860 by the German pathologist Carl von Rokitansky, the histopathologic finding was termed “cystosarcoma adenoids uterinum.” [1] Presenting signs and symptoms vary, but most commonly are painful menses and/or heavy menstrual bleeding. Historically, the condition was a histologic diagnosis that required biopsy or more often hysterectomy. Presently, the diagnosis can be made non-invasively using ultrasound or magnetic resonance imaging (MRI). The definitive treatment for women who no longer desire pregnancy is hysterectomy, while a variety of other medical and minimally invasive therapies are available for those who want to preserve fertility or want to avoid more extensive surgery.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 5 -- Community 2938
retrieved_docs.content: Female infertility (العقم لدى الإناث) and adenomyosis (انتباذ بطاني رحمي) are both gynecologic conditions for which epidemiological information is available, highlighting the importance of population‑level data in understanding their prevalence and risk factors. Epidemiology provides insight into how common each disorder is and which groups are most affected. Moreover, adenomyosis is directly related to female infertility, indicating that the presence of adenomyotic tissue within the uterine wall can contribute to difficulties conceiving. Recognizing the epidemiological patterns of both conditions and their interrelationship aids clinicians in identifying at‑risk patients, guiding diagnostic evaluation, and informing management strategies aimed at improving reproductive outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 4 -- Community 2806
retrieved_docs.content: Allowed qualifier is identified as a diagnostic label for both العقم لدى الإناث (female infertility) and انتباذ بطاني رحمي (uterine adenomyosis). The two conditions are related, with uterine adenomyosis being a potential contributor to female infertility. Recognizing that Allowed qualifier can be applied to each condition and understanding the relationship between العقم لدى الإناث and انتباذ بطاني رحمي supports more comprehensive assessment and management of affected patients.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 3 -- Community 2635

Endometriosis is a disease that is located in the uterus, reflecting the presence of ectopic endometrial tissue within or on the uterine structure. Both concepts highlight the uterus as a central focus for therapeutic interventions and pathological conditions, underscoring the importance of uterine health in patient care.

Understanding that endometriosis originates from tissue associated with the uterus is essential for accurate diagnosis, targeted treatment, and comprehensive patient care.

Together, these procedures represent specialized diagnostic techniques used to evaluate the cervix, vagina, uterus, and surrounding pelvic structures in female patients, facilitating accurate assessment and management of gynecologic conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 4 -- Community 2845
retrieved_docs.content: The female reproductive system encompasses surgical interventions such as abdominal hysterectomy, a procedure in which the uterus is removed through an abdominal incision. Abdominal hysterectomy frequently co‑occurs with salpingo‑oophorectomy, the removal of the fallopian tube and ovary, indicating that these gynecologic surgeries are often performed together. This combined approach is commonly employed in the management of conditions affecting the uterus and adnexal structures, such as malignant or benign tumors, severe endometriosis, or other complex pelvic diseases, and informs surgical planning and patient counseling in women’s health care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 3 -- Community 1410
retrieved_docs.content: Noninflammatory disorder of the female genital organs refers to a group of gynecologic conditions that are not caused by infection or inflammation. Within this category, two notable manifestations are كيسة رحمية (uterine cyst) and نزيف مهبلي (vaginal bleeding). Both entities are considered clinical outcomes or presentations that can arise from noninflammatory pathology of the female reproductive system, and recognizing them as part of the same disorder spectrum aids clinicians in diagnosis, monitoring, and management of affected patients.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 3 -- Community 2070
retrieved_docs.content: The urogenital system is closely linked to specific gynecologic conditions, including ovarian torsion and fallopian tube disorder. Ovarian torsion involves the twisting of the ovary, while fallopian tube disorder refers to abnormalities of the tubal structure. Both conditions are associated with the anatomic sites of the urogenital system, highlighting the importance of evaluating this system when diagnosing and managing ovarian and tubal pathologies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 4 -- Community 2734
retrieved_docs.content: Female Reproductive System Disorder is a broad clinical category that includes a range of conditions affecting the female reproductive organs. Within this category, cancer of the uterus represents a malignant neoplasm of the uterine tissue, while ورم مهبلي حميد (benign vaginal tumor) denotes a non‑malignant growth in the vagina. Recognizing both uterine cancer and benign vaginal tumors as specific manifestations of Female Reproductive System Disorder highlights their shared relevance to gynecologic health and underscores the importance of tailored diagnostic and therapeutic strategies for each condition.
